questions.text: I would relocate to be closer to someone who needs my daily support.
I am comfortable pulling rank to resolve a disagreement.
I keep things simple and deal with failures as they happen rather than building in redundancy.
I click around new software to figure it out through exploration.
When something excites me, the rush of energy is almost overwhelming.
I hold the view that the capacity to evaluate distressing situations without being emotionally overwhelmed is a hallmark of mature professional judgment.
I hold the view that the ability to analyze painful situations without emotional interference is a cognitive asset that grows more valuable with each difficult experience.
I tend to feel emotionally hungover the day after an intense experience.
I hold the view that my financial independence is one of the most important things I can achieve.
I believe the measure of my day is how much I gave, not how much I gained.
I routinely lend my possessions and rarely ask for them back.
I notice when people speak differently depending on who else is listening.
I proofread every piece of writing, even casual messages, before sending.
I believe that indirectness in communication wastes everyone's time.
I hold the view that soliciting everyone's input is a form of respect.
I find that my analytical temperament during emotionally charged situations has enabled me to navigate conflicts and dilemmas that would otherwise have been paralyzing.
I experience physical sensations from emotions — butterflies, chest tightness, flushing — only rarely.
I track recurring expenses and review them monthly to stay within my targets.
I believe that a leader who does not listen loses the group's trust.
I blend research, creation, and revision together fluidly rather than working in distinct phases.
I feel that I am at my best when I make my own interests a top priority.
I hold the view that my emotional stability is not indifference — it's simply a different calibration.
I believe that the decision to take someone's suffering into account — to let it register as important — is the beginning of any genuine moral action.
I notice when someone's handshake lingers a beat too long or ends a beat too soon.
I prefer to resolve disagreements through dialogue rather than any form of rank.
My internal state has a steadiness that I've come to recognize as simply the way I'm built.
I believe that my personal comfort zone should expand on my terms, not because someone else pushed me.
I weigh the trade-offs of a decision on paper before committing.
I do not naturally pick up on when someone changes the way they address me.
I readily step in to mediate disputes by imposing a resolution.
I hold the view that the moral concern I experience for those in suffering is the clearest evidence I have that I am living in accordance with my values.
I like to personally approve important outputs before they go out.
My emotional responses often spill over into adjacent situations — one upset bleeds into the next.
I do not naturally sense when someone is testing boundaries.
I hold the view that treating others' suffering as genuinely urgent is the most fundamental test of whether my moral commitments are real.
I can tell when someone asks how you are doing because they genuinely want to know versus when it is a formality.
